title: Ensure Classes that Override 'clone' are 'Cloneable' and Call 'super.clone()'
description: Cloneable is the marker Interface that indicates that clone() may be called on an object. Overriding clone() without implementing Cloneable can be helpful if you want to control how subclasses clone themselves, but otherwise, it's probably a mistake. The standard convention for Object.clone() according to Oracle's Javadoc is: x.clone() != x, x.clone().getClass() == x.getClass(), and x.clone().equals(x). Calling super.clone() helps to satisfy these invariants: it returns a new object instance and an object of the same type as the one clone() was called on. Object.clone() performs a shallow copy of the object's state.
recommendation: Implement 'Cloneable' on classes that override 'clone' and ensure 'super.clone()' is called within the overridden 'clone()' method.
impact: Improper implementation of 'clone' method could lead to unpredictable behaviour and inconsistencies in the application.

checks.message: Classes that override clone should implement Cloneable
